paragraphs.text: “And it was told Joseph: Behold, thy father is sick: and he took his two sons” (Gen 48:1) and brought them to Jacob, that they might receive his blessing before his death. “And Jacob said: God Almighty appeared unto me at Luz… when I slept with a stone for my pillow, and blessed me. And he said unto me: Behold… I will make of thee a multitude of nations (that is, into tribes). And now Ephraim and Manasseh, even as Reuben and Simeon, shall be mine. And the sons whom thou begettest after them shall be called by the names of the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh” (Gen 48:3–6). “And he said: Bring them unto me, and I will bless them” (Gen 48:9). And blessing the firstborn Manasseh, Israel “crossed his hands, and laid his right hand upon the head of Ephraim, who was the younger” (Gen 48:14). By this he plainly traced the cross, in the image of that mystery that the firstborn Israel would be diminished, like the firstborn Manasseh, while the heathen nations would be exalted, like the younger Ephraim. Blessing the children, Jacob said: “God bless these children, and let my name be named on them, and the name of my fathers” (Gen 48:16) — that is, let them be called the sons of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. Joseph tried to put his father’s right hand on Manasseh’s head, but Jacob would not, and said: “He also shall not be deprived of a blessing, and Manasseh shall be great, but his younger brother shall be greater than he” (Gen 48:19). And showing the preeminence of the younger over the elder, he added: “In thee shall Israel bless, saying: God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh” (Gen 48:20).

“And he said… to Joseph: I have given to thee… one portion above thy brethren, which I took out of the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow” (Gen 48:21–22) — that is, the portion which I bought for a hundred sheep (Gen 33:19), gained by my shepherd’s labors. Blessing the one born of Rachel, Jacob with sorrow recalls the death of Rachel, the cause of which was the bearing of the son.
